subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 19, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Hospital-Level Variation in Early Tracheostomy and Withdrawal
sentences: Story 1 is about Hospital-Level Variation in Early Tracheostomy and Withdrawal of Life-Sustaining Treatment in Severe Traumatic Brain Injury: A Nationwide Analysis..
This is a recent research article from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

A mixed-effects logistic regression model was developed to estimate each hospital's unique risk-adjusted odds ratio (AOR) for ET (≤ 7 d after injury), and hospitals were stratified into low-, medium-, and high-tendency groups based on the AOR for ET.
Among 22,156 patients with severe TBI treated at 417 hospitals, the ET rates were 16.8%, 30.1%, and 47.7% in the low-, medium-, and high-tendency hospitals, respectively.
WLST following tracheostomy occurred in 2.6%, 4.8%, and 9.6% of patients 20-39, 40-59, and 60-89 years old, respectively.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Red Blood Cell Transfusion Characteristics and Morbidity or Mo
sentences: Story 2 is about Red Blood Cell Transfusion Characteristics and Morbidity or Mortality in Very-Low-Birth-Weight Infants..
This is a recent research article from JAMA network open.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
The study enrolled 2605 VLBW infants, and 1283 (586 [45.7%] female; 713 [55.6%] <27 weeks' gestational age) received RBC transfusion.
Median pretransfusion hemoglobin level was associated with higher odds of the composite outcome (odds ratio [OR] per 1 g/dL increase, 1.15; 95% CI, 1.07-1.25; P < .001).

In contrast, use of additive solution (AS)-1 or AS-5 vs the reference of citrate phosphate dextrose adenine or citrate phosphate dextrose as the anticoagulant preservative solution was associated with lower odds of the composite outcome (OR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.53-0.97; P = .03).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Global environmental and geo-economic impact of conservative v
sentences: Story 3 is about Global environmental and geo-economic impact of conservative versus liberal oxygen strategies in mechanically ventilated critically ill adults: an ecological country-level analysis..
This is a recent research article from British journal of anaesthesia.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Global environmental and geo-economic impact of conservative compared with liberal oxygen strategies in mechanically ventilated critically ill adults: an ecological country-level analysis?
Cleaner grids would reduce global oxygen-related emissions by 49.4%-91.9%.

Adopting conservative rather than liberal SpO2 targets for ventilated ICU patients would reduce global annual expenditures from 180 to 76 million int$ (Δint$104 million) and cut emissions from 884 to 368 ktCO2eq (Δ516 ktCO2eq).

Oxygen-related costs were lower in countries with higher Gross Domestic Product per capita but higher in those with greater exposure to fine airborne particles (PM2.5) and higher out-of-pocket health spending.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Hospital-Level Variation in Early Tracheostomy and Withdrawal of Life-Sustaining Treatment in Severe Traumatic Brain Injury: A Nationwide Analysis.
articles.summary: A mixed-effects logistic regression model was developed to estimate each hospital's unique risk-adjusted odds ratio (AOR) for ET (≤ 7 d after injury), and hospitals were stratified into low-, medium-, and high-tendency groups based on the AOR for ET. Among 22,156 patients with severe TBI treated at 417 hospitals, the ET rates were 16.8%, 30.1%, and 47.7% in the low-, medium-, and high-tendency hospitals, respectively. WLST following tracheostomy occurred in 2.6%, 4.8%, and 9.6% of patients 20-39, 40-59, and 60-89 years old, respectively. After multilevel case-mix adjustments, a high hospital-level tendency for ET was associated with increased odds of WLST post-tracheostomy (AOR, 1.35; 95% CI, 1.10-1.66; p = 0.004), with the highest point estimate observed among patients 40-59 years old (AOR, 1.39; 95% CI, 1.01-1.91). Early tracheostomy (ET) in severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) is often considered during periods of prognostic uncertainty and evolving goals of care.
articles.analysis: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

The association between hospital-level tendency for ET and WLST post-tracheostomy was assessed. Among...

keyResults: A mixed-effects logistic regression model was developed to estimate each hospital's unique risk-adjusted odds ratio (AOR) for ET (≤ 7 d after injury), and hospitals were stratified into low-, medium-, and high-tendency groups based on the AOR for ET.
Among 22,156 patients with severe TBI treated at 417 hospitals, the ET rates were 16.8%, 30.1%, and 47.7% in the low-, medium-, and high-tendency hospitals, respectively.
WLST following tracheostomy occurred in 2.6%, 4.8%, and 9.6% of patients 20-39, 40-59, and 60-89 years old, respectively.

After multilevel case-mix adjustments, a high hospital-level tendency for ET was associated with increased odds of WLST post-tracheostomy (AOR, 1.35; 95% CI, 1.10-1.66; p = 0.004), with the highest point estimate observed among patients 40-59 years old (AOR, 1.39; 95% CI, 1.01-1.91).
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.

articles.title: Red Blood Cell Transfusion Characteristics and Morbidity or Mortality in Very-Low-Birth-Weight Infants.
articles.summary: To determine whether blood donor, RBC modifications and storage, or transfusion thresholds and characteristics are associated with serious adverse outcomes in VLBW infants undergoing transfusion. The primary outcome was a composite outcome of severe intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH), necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC), late-onset sepsis, severe bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD), retinopathy of prematurity, or death (and secondary individual outcomes), with follow-up through 90 days or death. Blood donor characteristics (sex, age, and hemoglobin), anticoagulant preservative solution and/or unit characteristics, transfused volumes, transfusion number, and infants' pretransfusion hemoglobin values were evaluated using multivariable generalized estimating equation regression models to account for correlation within hospital, adjusted for illness severity. The study enrolled 2605 VLBW infants, and 1283 (586 [45.7%] female; 713 [55.6%] <27 weeks' gestational age) received RBC transfusion. Median pretransfusion hemoglobin level was associated with higher odds of the composite outcome (odds ratio [OR] per 1 g/dL increase, 1.15; 95% CI, 1.07-1.25; P < .001).

Blood donor characteristics (sex, age, and hemoglobin),...

keyResults: The study enrolled 2605 VLBW infants, and 1283 (586 [45.7%] female; 713 [55.6%] <27 weeks' gestational age) received RBC transfusion.
Median pretransfusion hemoglobin level was associated with higher odds of the composite outcome (odds ratio [OR] per 1 g/dL increase, 1.15; 95% CI, 1.07-1.25; P < .001).

In contrast, use of additive solution (AS)-1 or AS-5 vs the reference of citrate phosphate dextrose adenine or citrate phosphate dextrose as the anticoagulant preservative solution was associated with lower odds of the composite outcome (OR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.53-0.97; P = .03).

Among secondary outcomes, anticoagulant preservative solution was associated with lower risk of BPD (AS-1 and AS-5, OR, 0.48; 95% CI, 0.33-0.69; AS-3, OR, 0.65; 95% CI, 0.56-0.77) and higher risk of NEC (AS-3, hazard ratio [HR], 5.33; 95% CI, 1.22-23.29).
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.

articles.title: Global environmental and geo-economic impact of conservative versus liberal oxygen strategies in mechanically ventilated critically ill adults: an ecological country-level analysis.
articles.summary: Conservative (peripheral O2 saturation, SpO2 88-92%) and liberal (SpO2 ≥96%) oxygenation targets were selected using data from a randomised trial. Cleaner grids would reduce global oxygen-related emissions by 49.4%-91.9%. Although conservative and liberal oxygenation targets yield similar clinical outcomes in mechanically ventilated critically ill adults, the global environmental and economic implications remain unknown. We conducted an ecological analysis using 2024 country-level aggregate data for 200 countries. We quantified total oxygen consumption costs (2024 international dollars [int$]) and associated greenhouse gas emissions (ktCO2eq) by integrating country-specific electricity grids and oxygen sources with parameters from a life-cycle assessment of medical oxygen.

pico.outcomes: Adopting conservative rather than liberal SpO2 targets for ventilated ICU patients would reduce global annual expenditures from 180 to 76 million int$ (Δint$104 million) and cut emissions from 884 to 368 ktCO2eq (Δ516 ktCO2eq). Oxygen-related costs were lower in countries with higher Gross Domestic Product per capita but higher in those with greater...

keyResults: Cleaner grids would reduce global oxygen-related emissions by 49.4%-91.9%.

Adopting conservative rather than liberal SpO2 targets for ventilated ICU patients would reduce global annual expenditures from 180 to 76 million int$ (Δint$104 million) and cut emissions from 884 to 368 ktCO2eq (Δ516 ktCO2eq).

Oxygen-related costs were lower in countries with higher Gross Domestic Product per capita but higher in those with greater exposure to fine airborne particles (PM2.5) and higher out-of-pocket health spending.
Per-ICU bed greenhouse gas emissions were highest in the Eastern Mediterranean, Central Asia, and upper-middle-income countries.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
